prior.title: The Energy Efficiency (Private Rented Property) (England and Wales) Regulations 2015
prior.summary: Secondary legislation establishing mandatory energy efficiency measures for private rented properties in England and Wales. Comprises 48 provisions setting standards and compliance requirements for rental property owners. Document is a Draft Statutory Instrument that has been superseded by a later version (ISBN 9780111132432).
prior.full_brief:
# Regulatory Fact Document: The Energy Efficiency (Private Rented Property) (England and Wales) Regulations 2015

---

# 1. Purpose and Scope of This Document

This document covers The Energy Efficiency (Private Rented Property) (England and Wales) Regulations 2015 (SI 2015/962), commonly referred to as the Minimum Energy Efficiency Standards (MEES) Regulations, as amended by SI 2016/660. It synthesises the current in-force position, the active forward-reform consultation cycle, and the proposed 2030 uplift announced in January 2026.

**Conventions:**
- All binding obligations are cited verbatim from source content or labeled with a provenance token. No obligation is asserted without a source span or label.
- Items requiring legal interpretation are marked *Legal Confirmation Required:*
- Analytical and inferential statements are prefixed with their label token.
- Transport mode and cargo vertical filters are applied where the workspace profile intersects with the regulation's scope.
- Dates are stated as facts where sourced. Prospective dates labeled as government proposals are distinguished from in-force milestones.
- This document reflects the regulatory position as of May 2025 for the in-force baseline, and the January–March 2026 consultation announcements for the forward picture.

**Scope note:** This regulation governs privately rented property in England and Wales. It does not apply to Scotland or Northern Ireland, which have separate regimes. It applies to landlords of domestic and non-domestic private rented properties. Workspaces in the role of freight forwarder, logistics operator, or cargo principal are not primary obligated parties under this regulation. However, workspaces that occupy, sublet, or hold leasehold interests in warehousing, depot, or office premises in England and Wales as tenants or landlords are within scope as either landlords or affected tenants.

---

# 2. What This Regulation Is and Why It Applies to the Workspace

**What the regulation is:**

The Energy Efficiency (Private Rented Property) (England and Wales) Regulations 2015 (SI 2015/962) established minimum energy efficiency standards (MEES) for domestic and non-domestic private rented properties in England and Wales. *Source: The Energy Efficiency (Private Rented Property) (England and Wales) Regulations 2015, SI 2015/962, legislation.gov.uk, 2015. https://www.legislation.gov.uk/uksi/2015/962/contents/made

"The Energy Efficiency (Private Rented Property) (England and Wales) Regulations 2015 introduced minimum energy efficiency standards (MEES) for domestic private rented properties in 2018." *Source: Minimum Energy Efficiency Standards, Wealden District Council, undated. https://www.wealden.gov.uk/housing/private-housing/mees/

"The Regulations set the minimum energy efficiency rating for all private rented properties and make it unlawful for properties with an energy performance rating of F or G to be let." *Source: The Energy Efficiency (Private Rented Property) (England and Wales) Regulations 2015 – overview, The Compliance People, undated. https://thecompliancepeople.co.uk/updates/news/energy-efficiency-regulations-2015/

"The Energy Efficiency (Private Rented Property) (England and Wales) Regulations 2015 establish a minimum level of energy efficiency for rented property in England and Wales." *Source: Non-domestic private rented property: minimum energy efficiency standard – landlord guidance, Department for Energy Security and Net Zero (DESNZ), published 13 August 2019, last updated 5 May 2026. https://www.gov.uk/guidance/non-domestic-private-rented-property-minimum-energy-efficiency-standard-landlord-guidance

An Energy Performance Certificate (EPC) rates energy performance on a scale of A (most efficient) to G (least efficient). "An EPC gives a property an energy efficiency rating from A (most efficient) to a G (least efficient) and is valid for 10 years." *Source: Minimum Energy Efficiency Standards, Wealden District Council. https://www.wealden.gov.uk/housing/private-housing/mees/

**Current in-force minimum standard:**

The current minimum is EPC band E for all domestic and non-domestic private rented properties. "Currently, privately rented properties must achieve an Energy Performance Certificate (EPC) rating of E or better or have a valid exemption registered. The legislation prevents landlords from renting out a property with a rating of F or G. This applies to all new and existing tenancies." *Source: Minimum Energy Efficiency Standards, Wealden District Council. https://www.wealden.gov.uk/housing/private-housing/mees/

**Effective date and in-force milestones:**

The regulations were made in 2015. "In March 2015 the government passed The Energy Efficiency (Private Rented Property) (England and Wales) Regulations 2015." *Source: The Compliance People. https://thecompliancepeople.co.uk/updates/news/energy-efficiency-regulations-2015/

"On 1st April 2016 parts 1 and 2 of the Regulations came into force imposing a new right for tenants of domestic private rented property." *Source: The Compliance People. https://thecompliancepeople.co.uk/updates/news/energy-efficiency-regulations-2015/

"From 1st April 2018, a landlord of a private rented property with an EPC rating of F or G must not grant a new lease or renew an existing lease of that property until works have been carried out to improve the energy efficiency to a rating of E or above." *Source: The Compliance People. https://thecompliancepeople.co.uk/updates/news/energy-efficiency-regulations-2015/

"From 1st April 2020, a landlord of a domestic private rented property with an EPC rating of F or G must not continue to let the property until works have been carried out to improve the energy efficiency rating to an E or above." *Source: The Compliance People. https://thecompliancepeople.co.uk/updates/news/energy-efficiency-regulations-2015/

"From 1st April 2023, a landlord of a non-domestic private rented property with an EPC rating of F or G must not continue to let the property until works have been carried out to improve the energy efficiency rating to an E or above." *Source: The Compliance People. https://thecompliancepeople.co.uk/updates/news/energy-efficiency-regulations-2015/

**Why it applies to the workspace:**

Operational implication: Workspaces in a global freight forwarding operation that lease or own non-domestic premises — including warehouses, depots, temperature-controlled facilities, offices, and bonded stores — in England and Wales are affected as tenants where their landlord is subject to MEES, and as landlords where they sublet or hold leasehold interests in qualifying premises. A non-domestic lease for a term certain of between 6 months and 99 years on a property legally required to have an EPC brings the property within scope. *Legal Confirmation Required:* Whether a specific workspace premises arrangement constitutes a "relevant tenancy" under the Regulations, and whether the workspace acts as landlord or tenant in that arrangement, requires counsel and facilities management review.

---

# 3. Issues Requiring Immediate Action

### MONITORING — Non-Domestic Premises EPC Status Check

**Action:** Audit all England and Wales non-domestic premises (warehouses, depots, offices, bonded stores) currently held under leases of between 6 months and 99 years to confirm EPC ratings are E or above, or that valid exemptions are registered.

**Why now:** "From 1 April 2023, the requirement for non-domestic landlords to obtain at least an EPC E rating, unless they have registered a valid exemption, applies to all privately rented non-domestic properties (even where there has been no change in tenancy)." *Source: DESNZ Non-domestic landlord guidance. https://www.gov.uk/guidance/non-domestic-private-rented-property-minimum-energy-efficiency-standard-landlord-guidance

This milestone is in force as of 1 April 2023. Operational implication: Any non-domestic workspace premises held on a relevant tenancy with an EPC rating of F or G is currently in a breach position if the landlord has not registered a valid exemption.

**Cost exposure:** Non-domestic penalties reach up to £150,000 per property for a letting period of 3 months or more (see Section 8). Current rate publicly available per source content.

---

### MONITORING — Forward 2030 MEES Uplift Planning

**Action:** Begin property-level energy efficiency gap analysis across all England and Wales premises against the proposed EPC C standard, to identify which properties will require capital investment before the proposed 2030 deadline.

**Why now:** "On 21 January 2026, the government confirmed that private landlords in England and Wales will need to ensure their properties meet an EPC C by 1 October 2030 for all tenancies, unless they have an exemption." *Source: The proposed new EPC rules for landlords, The Independent Landlord (Suzanne Smith), 12 March 2026. https://theindependentlandlord.com/new-epc-rules/

Analytical inference: The 2030 deadline, if legislated as proposed, gives a planning window of approximately four years from the date of this brief. For workspaces with large non-domestic portfolios in England and Wales, early identification of sub-C-rated properties allows phased capital expenditure planning and avoids compressed upgrade timelines.

---

### MONITORING — EPC Consultation Outcome (December 2024 / February 2025 Consultations)

**Action:** Track the outcome of the December 2024 consultation on reform to the Energy Performance of Buildings regime (closed 26 February 2025) and the February 2025 consultation on improving the energy performance of privately rented homes, and monitor secondary legislation that will implement the new MEES framework.

"On 4th December 2024, the UK Government released its plan for a consultation on the reform to the Energy Performance of Building regime, which will run from 4th December 2024 until 26th February 2025." *Source: Current and Proposed MEES Regulations 2025, Easy EPC, undated. https://www.easyepc.org/mees-legislation-updates-current-and-proposed-changes

Analytical inference: Re-check window: the next authoritative signal is expected when the government publishes its full consultation response and introduces secondary legislation. The Home Energy Model (HEM) launch has been delayed to "the second half of 2027". *Source: The Independent Landlord. https://theindependentlandlord.com/new-epc-rules/

---

# 4. How the Workspace Sits in the Compliance Chain

**Jurisdictional scope:**

The regulations apply to England and Wales only. *Source: DESNZ Non-domestic landlord guidance. https://www.gov.uk/guidance/non-domestic-private-rented-property-minimum-energy-efficiency-standard-landlord-guidance

Operational implication: Scotland and Northern Ireland are outside this regulation's scope. Premises in those jurisdictions are governed by separate regimes not covered by this brief.

**Property coverage — non-domestic:**

"The Regulations apply to all non-domestic rented properties that are legally required to have an Energy Performance Certificate (EPC)." *Source: DESNZ Non-domestic landlord guidance. https://www.gov.uk/guidance/non-domestic-private-rented-property-minimum-energy-efficiency-standard-landlord-guidance

"The regulations do not apply to non-domestic rented properties that are either: granted for a 'term certain' (the original term or time period granted by a tenancy) not exceeding 6 months ... granted for a 'term certain' of 99 years or more." *Source: DESNZ Non-domestic landlord guidance. https://www.gov.uk/guidance/non-domestic-private-rented-property-minimum-energy-efficiency-standard-landlord-guidance

**Workspace role placement:**

Workspaces in a freight forwarding operation typically occupy England and Wales premises under two roles:

1. **As tenant:** Where the workspace leases warehousing, depot, or office space from a third-party landlord on a relevant tenancy, the landlord is the obligated party under MEES. Operational implication: A workspace as tenant benefits from the regulation's requirement that its landlord maintain the property at EPC E (current) or EPC C (proposed 2030), but the workspace itself is not directly fined. However, a landlord who cannot comply may face restrictions on renewal or continuation of the tenancy, which creates operational continuity risk for the workspace.

2. **As landlord:** Where the workspace sublets premises, or holds a headlease and grants underleases to third parties, the workspace is in the role of landlord and is the directly obligated party subject to penalties. *Legal Confirmation Required:* Whether the workspace holds any interest that places it in the role of "landlord" under the Regulations for any England and Wales premises requires facilities management and legal confirmation on a property-by-property basis.

**EPC requirements:**

"An Energy Performance Certificate (EPC) is needed whenever a property is built, sold or rented." *Source: Minimum Energy Efficiency Standards, Wealden District Council. https://www.wealden.gov.uk/housing/private-housing/mees/

Operational implication: Where the workspace takes a new lease on non-domestic premises in England and Wales, the landlord is required to provide an EPC before marketing or letting. The workspace should request and retain the EPC at lease commencement and at each renewal.

---

# 5. Anticipated Authoritative Guidance and Pending Regulatory Events

**MONITORING**

### Event 1: Home Energy Model (HEM) Launch — delayed to second half of 2027

"Government continues to work at pace to deliver reformed domestic EPCs. Following engagement with industry on the delivery timeline, we have decided to move the launch of the reforms to the second half of 2027." *Source: The Independent Landlord, citing the government's "outcome update" of 9 March 2026. https://theindependentlandlord.com/new-epc-rules/

**What to expect:** The HEM will replace the Standard Assessment Procedure (SAP) and RdSAP as the methodology for EPC assessments. "The HEM will replace the Standard Assessment Procedure (SAP) and Reduced data SAP (RdSAP), which use the cost based Energy Efficiency Rating (EER) model, and become compulsory to use for EPCs from 1 October 2029." *Source: The Independent Landlord. https://theindependentlandlord.com/new-epc-rules/

**Current phase/status:** Consultation on HEM:EPC metrics launched 21 January 2026; the metrics have not yet been finalised as of the date of this brief. *Source: The Independent Landlord. https://theindependentlandlord.com/new-epc-rules/

**Re-check window:** Monitor for HEM launch date confirmation and finalised metric boundaries from summer 2027 onwards.

---

### Event 2: Secondary legislation to implement EPC C / 2030 MEES uplift

"On 21 January 2026, the government confirmed that private landlords in England and Wales will need to ensure their properties meet an EPC C by 1 October 2030 for all tenancies, unless they have an exemption." *Source: The Independent Landlord. https://theindependentlandlord.com/new-epc-rules/

**What to expect:** Secondary legislation will be required to amend SI 2015/962 to give legal effect to the EPC C standard, the revised cost cap, revised exemptions, and revised penalty levels. None of these are yet in force.

**Current phase/status:** Government proposals published January 2026 as part of the Warm Homes Plan. Consultations on EPC reform (December 2024) and MEES for private rented sector (February 2025) have closed. Full government response pending.

Analytical inference: Based on the stated 1 October 2030 target for all tenancies, secondary legislation would need to be enacted and commence no later than mid-2029 to give landlords meaningful lead time. The expected re-check window for draft secondary legislation is 2027–2028.

---

### Event 3: EPC Consultation on Energy Performance of Buildings Reform (December 2024 – February 2025)

"On 4th December 2024, the UK Government released its plan for a consultation on the reform to the Energy Performance of Building regime, which will run from 4th December 2024 until 26th February 2025." Topics included updating EPC metrics, reducing validity periods, and requiring landlords to hold a valid EPC for the duration of a tenancy. *Source: Easy EPC. https://www.easyepc.org/mees-legislation-updates-current-and-proposed-changes

**Status:** Consultation closed 26 February 2025. The government published partial responses on 21 January 2026. Full legislative response pending. *Source: The Independent Landlord. https://theindependentlandlord.com/new-epc-rules/

---

# 6. Threshold Questions

### Question 1: Is the workspace's premises a "domestic" or "non-domestic" private rented property?

**Status — decided by fact:** Freight forwarding warehouses, depots, and logistics facilities are non-domestic. The non-domestic timeline and penalty schedule applies. *Source: DESNZ Non-domestic landlord guidance. https://www.gov.uk/guidance/non-domestic-private-rented-property-minimum-energy-efficiency-standard-landlord-guidance

---

### Question 2: Is the property "legally required to have an EPC"?

**Status — decided by fact for most operational premises:** "If the property you let has been marketed for sale or let, or modified, in the past 10 years then it will probably be legally required to have an EPC." *Source: DESNZ Non-domestic landlord guidance. https://www.gov.uk/guidance/non-domestic-private-rented-property-minimum-energy-efficiency-standard-landlord-guidance

*Legal Confirmation Required:* Whether any specific workspace premises that have not been marketed or modified in the past 10 years are exempt from EPC requirements requires property-by-property legal and facilities review.

---

### Question 3: Is the tenancy for a term certain of between 6 months and 99 years?

**Status — Legal Confirmation Required:** The exclusions for tenancies of under 6 months and 99 years or more are stated in the regulations. *Source: DESNZ Non-domestic landlord guidance. https://www.gov.uk/guidance/non-domestic-private-rented-property-minimum-energy-efficiency-standard-landlord-guidance. Whether any specific workspace lease falls outside this range requires lease-by-lease review by counsel.

---

### Question 4: Does the workspace hold the premises in the role of landlord for any sublet arrangement?

**Status — Legal Confirmation Required:** See Section 4 above. Role as landlord triggers direct obligations and penalty exposure.

---

# 7. Substantive Requirements

## 7a. Current in-force minimum standard — Domestic (in force as of 1 April 2020)

"From 1st April 2020, a landlord of a domestic private rented property with an EPC rating of F or G must not continue to let the property until works have been carried out to improve the energy efficiency rating to an E or above." *Source: The Compliance People. https://thecompliancepeople.co.uk/updates/news/energy-efficiency-regulations-2015/

Operational implication: For any domestic residential premises held by the workspace as landlord (e.g. staff accommodation, residential property sublet), the E rating floor has applied to all continuing lettings since 1 April 2020.

## 7b. Current in-force minimum standard — Non-Domestic (in force as of 1 April 2023)

"From 1 April 2023, the requirement for non-domestic landlords to obtain at least an EPC E rating, unless they have registered a valid exemption, applies to all privately rented non-domestic properties (even where there has been no change in tenancy)." *Source: DESNZ Non-domestic landlord guidance. https://www.gov.uk/guidance/non-domestic-private-rented-property-minimum-energy-efficiency-standard-landlord-guidance

Operational implication: This is the most directly relevant provision for workspaces with non-domestic premises in England and Wales held as landlord. As of 1 April 2023, a continuing let below EPC E — absent a registered exemption — is a current breach.

## 7c. Tenant rights — energy efficiency improvements (in force as of 1 April 2016)

"On 1st April 2016 parts 1 and 2 of the Regulations came into force imposing a new right for tenants of domestic private rented property. Tenants can now request consent from their landlords to install energy efficiency improvement in the property they rent. The tenant is required to secure suitable funding for the requested improvements, and, if no exemptions apply, the landlord cannot unreasonably refuse consent." *Source: The Compliance People. https://thecompliancepeople.co.uk/updates/news/energy-efficiency-regulations-2015/

Analytical inference: For workspaces acting as tenant, this provision creates a right to request landlord consent for energy efficiency improvements — relevant where the workspace seeks to improve sustainability credentials in leased premises for emissions reporting purposes.

## 7d. EPC validity and renewal

"An EPC gives a property an energy efficiency rating from A (most efficient) to a G (least efficient) and is valid for 10 years." *Source: Wealden District Council. https://www.wealden.gov.uk/housing/private-housing/mees/

Analytical inference: Where the workspace holds leases on non-domestic premises, requesting and retaining the current EPC is the first operational step. EPCs older than 10 years are expired and a new assessment would be required before any exemption application.

## 7e. PRS Exemptions Register

"Where a landlord considers an exemption applies allowing them to let their property below an 'E' EPC rating, the landlord will have to log this on a centralised register — 'the Private Rented Sector (PRS) Exemptions Register.'" *Source: The Compliance People. https://thecompliancepeople.co.uk/updates/news/energy-efficiency-regulations-2015/

"Exemptions last for five years." *Source: The Compliance People. https://thecompliancepeople.co.uk/updates/news/energy-efficiency-regulations-2015/

"Where an exemption applies, the exemption must be registered by the landlord (or an agent for the landlord) before it can be relied on." *Source: DESNZ Non-domestic landlord guidance. https://www.gov.uk/guidance/non-domestic-private-rented-property-minimum-energy-efficiency-standard-landlord-guidance

"Please note: any exemptions from the prohibition on letting F or G rated property which are registered on the PRS Exemptions Register may not pass over to a new owner or landlord of a property upon sale, or other transfer." *Source: DESNZ Non-domestic landlord guidance. https://www.gov.uk/guidance/non-domestic-private-rented-property-minimum-energy-efficiency-standard-landlord-guidance

Operational implication: For workspaces acquiring leasehold interests in England and Wales properties currently registered as exempt, the exemption ceases on transfer and a new exemption or improvement to EPC E is required from the date of acquisition.

---

# 8. Exemptions and Edge Cases

**Available exemptions — non-domestic (current):**

1. **7-year payback exemption:** "The prohibition on letting a non-domestic property below an EPC energy efficiency rating of E does not apply if a landlord can show that the cost of purchasing and installing a recommended improvement or improvements does not meet a simple 7 year payback test." Evidence required: three installer quotes and cost calculation. This exemption lasts 5 years. *Source: DESNZ Non-domestic landlord guidance. https://www.gov.uk/guidance/non-domestic-private-rented-property-minimum-energy-efficiency-standard-landlord-guidance

2. **All improvements made exemption:** Where all relevant energy efficiency improvements have been made (or there are none that can be made) and the property remains below EPC E. Lasts 5 years. *Source: DESNZ Non-domestic landlord guidance. https://www.gov.uk/guidance/non-domestic-private-rented-property-minimum-energy-efficiency-standard-landlord-guidance

3. **Wall insulation exemption:** Available where the only relevant improvements are cavity wall, external wall, or internal wall insulation and expert written advice confirms these would negatively impact the fabric or structure. *Source: DESNZ Non-domestic landlord guidance. https://www.gov.uk/guidance/non-domestic-private-rented-property-minimum-energy-efficiency-standard-landlord-guidance

4. **Third-party consent exemption:** "A landlord is unable to obtain the necessary consent to install the required energy efficiency improvements, for example from the tenants, planning authority, lender, superior landlords, etc." *Source: The Compliance People. https://thecompliancepeople.co.uk/updates/news/energy-efficiency-regulations-2015/

5. **Property devaluation exemption:** "An independent surveyor determines that the improvement works would reduce the property's value by 5% or more, or could cause damage to the property." *Source: The Compliance People. https://thecompliancepeople.co.uk/updates/news/energy-efficiency-regulations-2015/

6. **Temporary exemption (recently becoming a landlord):** Available in certain circumstances defined in Regulation 33. *Source: SI 2015/962 Table of Contents, Regulation 33. https://www.legislation.gov.uk/uksi/2015/962/contents/made

**Listed buildings and conservation areas:**

"Although the Regulations do not directly mention that listed buildings are exempt from meeting the minimum standard, they do refer back to the Planning (Listed Buildings and Conservation Areas) Act 1990. The Act states that in order to carry out improvements on a listed building third party consent is needed e.g. you will need to put forward the proposed upgrades to the Local Authority for approval. If the third party refuses the upgrades, or the conditions they set can't be reasonably met, then the building will be exempt from having to make the changes." *Source: The Compliance People. https://thecompliancepeople.co.uk/updates/news/energy-efficiency-regulations-2015/

Operational implication: Workspaces occupying or holding interests in historic or listed properties in England and Wales (including fine art storage facilities or heritage logistics venues) should assess the listed building consent implications before undertaking any energy efficiency works.

**Proposed future exemptions under the 2030 uplift:**

The government is considering a new affordability exemption under the proposed 2030 MEES framework, and is reviewing whether existing exemptions remain suitable. A proposed cost cap of £15,000 (subject to consultation) would serve as a cap-based exemption. These are **not yet in force.** *Source: Elmhurst Energy, EPC C by 2030: Government Consults on Big Changes for Private Rented Sector, 7 February 2025. https://www.elmhurstenergy.co.uk/blog/2025/02/07/epc-c-by-2030-government-consults-on-big-changes-for-private-rented-sector/

---

# 9. Registration and Reporting Obligations

**PRS Exemptions Register — current obligation:**

Exemptions are self-certified and registered on the PRS Exemptions Register before a property below EPC E can be let or continue to be let. "This registration is made on a self-certification basis and an exemption will apply from the point at which it is registered." *Source: DESNZ Non-domestic landlord guidance. https://www.gov.uk/guidance/non-domestic-private-rented-property-minimum-energy-efficiency-standard-landlord-guidance

Information required for all exemptions: "address of the property, type of exemption you are registering, landlord details." *Source: DESNZ Non-domestic landlord guidance. https://www.gov.uk/guidance/non-domestic-private-rented-property-minimum-energy-efficiency-standard-landlord-guidance

**Penalties for false registration:**

"Penalties between £1,000 and £5,000 also apply where a landlord has registered false or misleading information to the PRS Exemption Register." *Source: The Compliance People. https://thecompliancepeople.co.uk/updates/news/energy-efficiency-regulations-2015/

**EPC obligation:**

"Landlords can be fined if they don't get an EPC when they need one." *Source: Wealden District Council. https://www.wealden.gov.uk/housing/private-housing/mees/

*Specific EPC penalty amount for failure to obtain an EPC not available as a verbatim figure from the provided source blocks.* See the EPC Regulations 2012 for this specific penalty. This is flagged as a research gap.

---

# 10. Penalty and Enforcement

### COST ALERT

**Non-domestic penalties (current, in force):**

"Up to £5000, or up to 10% of the rateable value of the property (whichever is most), with a maximum penalty of £5000 and a publication penalty for letting a non-compliant property for less than three months." *Source: Easy EPC. https://www.easyepc.org/mees-legislation-updates-current-and-proposed-changes

"Up to £10000, or up to 20% of the rateable value of the property (whichever is most), with a maximum penalty or £150000 and a publication penalty for letting a non-compliant property for three months or more." *Source: Easy EPC. https://www.easyepc.org/mees-legislation-updates-current-and-proposed-changes

"Up to £5000 and a publication penalty for registering false or misleading information to the PRS Exemptions Register." *Source: Easy EPC. https://www.easyepc.org/mees-legislation-updates-current-and-proposed-changes

"Up to £5000 and a publication penalty for failing to comply with a compliance notice." *Source: Easy EPC. https://www.easyepc.org/mees-legislation-updates-current-and-proposed-changes

**Domestic penalties (current, in force):**

"Up to £2000 and/or a publication penalty for letting a non-compliant property for less than 3 months." *Source: Easy EPC. https://www.easyepc.org/mees-legislation-updates-current-and-proposed-changes

"Up to £4000 and/or a publication penalty for letting a non-compliant property for 3 months or more." *Source: Easy EPC. https://www.easyepc.org/mees-legislation-updates-current-and-proposed-changes

"Up to £1000 and/or a publication penalty for providing false or misleading information on the PRS Exemptions Register." *Source: Easy EPC. https://www.easyepc.org/mees-legislation-updates-current-and-proposed-changes

"The maximum total amount that a landlord can be fined per property is £5000." [Domestic.] *Source: Easy EPC. https://www.easyepc.org/mees-legislation-updates-current-and-proposed-changes

"These are the maximum penalty amounts per property." [Non-domestic.] *Source: Easy EPC. https://www.easyepc.org/mees-legislation-updates-current-and-proposed-changes

**Publication penalty:** "A Publication Penalty means that a local authority can publish the following information on the PRS Exemptions Register: Details of the breach; The name of the landlord (providing that this is not an individual); The address of the property where the breach occurred; and The amount of the financial penalty imposed." *Source: Easy EPC. https://www.easyepc.org/mees-legislation-updates-current-and-proposed-changes

**Enforcement authority:**

"The Council is investigating any potential breaches of the MEES regulations and enforcement action will be considered against landlords that fail to bring their property up to the required standard." *Source: Wealden District Council. https://www.wealden.gov.uk/housing/private-housing/mees/

"A local authority can serve a financial penalty if they find out a property is or has been in breach of MEES Regulations up to 18 months after the breach and/or publish details of the breach for at least 12 months." *Source: Easy EPC. https://www.easyepc.org/mees-legislation-updates-current-and-proposed-changes

**Proposed future penalty level (not yet in force):**

"Under the current regulations the maximum amount landlords can be fined for not complying with MEES is £5,000 per property. The government's preferred position is that the fine level for future MEES standards is set at £30,000 per property, and per breach of the regulations." *Source: Elmhurst Energy. https://www.elmhurstenergy.co.uk/blog/2025/02/07/epc-c-by-2030-government-consults-on-big-changes-for-private-rented-sector/

Analytical inference: The proposed jump from £5,000 to £30,000 per property per breach represents a sixfold increase in domestic maximum penalties if the government's preferred position is enacted. For non-domestic properties, the £150,000 maximum is already in force; the proposed domestic uplift is the more material change for workspaces with residential sublets.

---

# 11. Operational System Requirements

**Gap analysis:**

Operational implication: For workspaces with no current EPC register for England and Wales premises, the primary operational gap is data: an inventory of all premises, their EPC ratings, EPC expiry dates, tenancy types, and tenancy term lengths is the foundational requirement before any compliance posture can be assessed.

**What the workspace must build or maintain:**

1. **EPC register:** A maintained schedule of all England and Wales non-domestic premises with: current EPC band, EPC expiry date, tenancy type, lease term, and landlord/tenant role. Scope: all premises held under leases of between 6 months and 99 years. Deadline: immediate — the E standard for non-domestic continuing lets has been in force since 1 April 2023.

2. **Exemption tracking:** Where any premises is rated F or G and an exemption has been registered on the PRS Exemptions Register, a tracking system to monitor exemption expiry dates (5-year validity). "This exemption will last 5 years. # Purpose and Scope of This Document

This document synthesises available sourced content about Germany's Federal Ministry for Digital and Transport (BMDV, now designated Federal Ministry of Transport / Bundesministerium für Verkehr, BMV) as a regulatory and policy authority relevant to freight forwarding operations active on German and European trade lanes. It covers the ministry's current leadership, active policy initiatives, and forward regulatory signals in the areas of aviation fuels, green shipping corridors, rail, and hydrogen infrastructure acceleration.

**Convention notes:** The ministry was formerly abbreviated BMDV; current branding on official channels uses BMV. Both refer to the same federal body. This document uses BMV/BMDV interchangeably to match source materials. The workspace is referenced throughout by operational profile, never by name. All inline source citations follow the format: *Source: [Title], [Issuing Body], [Date]. [URL].*

**Date of generation:** 2026-05-27.

---

# What This Regulatory Body Is and Why It Applies to the Workspace

The Federal Ministry of Transport (BMV) is the German federal authority responsible for aviation, road, rail, water/maritime, cycling, freight transport and logistics, and transport planning. It drives German domestic implementation of EU transport policy and represents Germany in international transport forums including the IMO and EU Council. *Source: BMV Homepage, Federal Ministry of Transport, 2026. https://bmdv.bund.de/EN/Home/home.html.

Its remit explicitly covers freight transport and logistics as a named topic cluster, including waterborne freight, dangerous goods, combined transport, and transport planning and investment. *Source: BMV Homepage, Federal Ministry of Transport, 2026. https://bmdv.bund.de/EN/Home/home.html.

For workspaces operating as freight forwarders on German corridors — with air as the primary mode and road/ocean as secondary/tertiary — BMV is the upstream policy authority for three active signals that intersect directly with cost, route planning, and client sustainability claims: (1) the eSAF market consultation launched May 2026, directly affecting air fuel economics; (2) Germany's accession to Canada's green shipping corridors initiative, affecting ocean routing choices; and (3) the hydrogen infrastructure acceleration law (WasserstoffBG), affecting ground and rail connectivity to key German logistics hubs.

*Legal Confirmation Required:* Whether any specific BMV policy instrument creates direct legal obligations on freight forwarders (as distinct from carriers, fuel suppliers, or vessel operators) requires counsel review for each instrument individually.

---

# Issues Requiring Immediate Action

**MONITORING — eSAF Market Consultation, launched 19 May 2026**

The Federal Ministry of Transport launched a market consultation on support for Renewable Aviation Fuel (eSAF) on 19 May 2026. *Source: BMV Homepage, Federal Ministry of Transport, 2026-05-19. https://bmdv.bund.de/EN/Home/home.html.

Analytical inference: For workspaces moving cargo by air through German airports, the outcome of this consultation will directly shape the domestic eSAF support framework — including whether eSAF purchase obligations, blend mandates, or financial support mechanisms are imposed on German aviation supply chains. Participating in or monitoring the consultation response window is the highest-value action available now, before the framework is set.

*Recommended action:* Identify the consultation response window (see Section 6 below) and, if the workspace has contracted air carriers operating through German airports, brief commercial leadership on the eSAF cost trajectory before rates are passed through as surcharges.

**MONITORING — Green Shipping Corridors (Canada–Germany)**

Germany became the first country to join Canada's initiative on green shipping corridors, announced 7 October 2025. *Source: BMV Homepage, Federal Ministry of Transport, 2025-10-07. https://bmdv.bund.de/EN/Home/home.html.

Analytical inference: For workspaces routing ocean freight on transatlantic lanes (Americas trade lane), this corridor initiative is an early signal that compliance requirements on specific route segments — reporting, fuel choice, or port-call standards — may follow as the corridor matures. No binding obligations have been sourced as of this date.

**MONITORING — Hydrogen Infrastructure Acceleration Law (WasserstoffBG)**

A law to accelerate the planning, permitting, production, storage, import and transport of hydrogen (Gesetz zur planungs- und genehmigungsrechtlichen Beschleunigung von Erzeugung, Speicherung, Import und Transport von Wasserstoff) is published on the official German legal gazette portal. *Source: WasserstoffBG, Gesetze im Internet / Federal Ministry of Justice, date not stated in source. https://www.gesetze-im-internet.de/wasserstoffbg/.

Analytical inference: For workspaces using intermodal road–rail combinations through German hubs, accelerated hydrogen infrastructure deployment affects the timeline for hydrogen-powered trucking and rail traction becoming commercially available as alternatives on German corridors.

---

# How the Workspace Sits in the Compliance Chain

The Federal Ministry of Transport issues policy, runs consultations, sets subsidy frameworks, and implements EU transport directives at the German national level. It does not directly regulate freight forwarders as a category — obligations flow through the instruments it administers (e.g., aviation fuel blend mandates under EU ReFuelEU Aviation, IMO GHG measures under MARPOL, hydrogen corridor permits under WasserstoffBG) and land on carriers, fuel suppliers, port operators, and infrastructure operators.

For workspaces operating as freight forwarders, the compliance chain is indirect:
- **Air mode:** eSAF costs originating from BMV-administered support frameworks will be passed through by air carriers as fuel surcharges. The forwarder is not the obligated party under aviation fuel law, but absorbs cost impact commercially.
- **Ocean mode:** Green corridor commitments shape carrier behaviour and port-call standards on affected route segments; forwarders selecting carriers on those corridors will face changed service availability and pricing.
- **Road/Rail (German corridors):** WasserstoffBG accelerates hydrogen infrastructure, which may affect future fleet options and cost structures for drayage and intermodal moves within Germany.

*Legal Confirmation Required:* The specific role of a freight forwarder as an obligated or affected party under each BMV-administered instrument (ReFuelEU Aviation, MARPOL Annex VI, WasserstoffBG) requires instrument-by-instrument legal review.

---

# Anticipated Authoritative Guidance and Pending Regulatory Events

**eSAF Market Consultation — response and outcome window**

The market consultation on Support for Renewable Aviation Fuel (eSAF) was launched on 19 May 2026. *Source: BMV Homepage, Federal Ministry of Transport, 2026-05-19. https://bmdv.bund.de/EN/Home/home.html.

*Specific consultation close date and publication schedule not available from primary sources as of 2026-05-27.* The source block confirms launch but does not state the response deadline or publication timeline for outcomes.

Analytical inference: German federal consultations of this type typically run 6–12 weeks from launch, suggesting a response window closing in July–August 2026 and outcomes publishable in late 2026 or Q1 2027. This estimate is not sourced and carries no binding status.

*Re-check window:* August 2026 — re-pull BMV publications page for consultation outcome or draft support framework publication.

**Germany–UK Direct Rail Link**

Germany and Great Britain intend to establish a direct rail link, announced 13 August 2025. *Source: BMV Homepage, Federal Ministry of Transport, 2025-08-13. https://bmdv.bund.de/EN/Home/home.html.

Analytical inference: For workspaces currently routing Europe–UK cargo via Dover–Calais road or via air, a direct rail link would open a new intermodal option on the Europe–UK corridor. No operational date has been sourced. This is an intent announcement, not a confirmed project with a binding schedule.

*Re-check window:* Q4 2026 — re-pull for feasibility study publication or bilateral agreement signing.

**IMO GHG Negotiations — German Position**

Germany's BMV published a statement on ongoing IMO negotiations on climate action measures in international maritime shipping on 9 September 2025. *Source: BMV Homepage, Federal Ministry of Transport, 2025-09-09. https://bmdv.bund.de/EN/Home/home.html.

Analytical inference: Germany's active engagement in IMO GHG negotiations signals that German-flagged vessel operators and German port-call economics will be shaped by IMO MEPC outcomes. For workspaces routing ocean cargo through Hamburg or Bremen, any IMO GHG levy or fuel standard adopted in 2025–2026 will affect carrier pricing on those port calls.

*Re-check window:* IMO MEPC 83 outcome (April 2025 already passed; next MEPC session timing should be confirmed from IMO source — not available in source blocks).

**Baltic/North Sea Coastal States Open Letter**

An open letter by coastal states of the Baltic Sea and the North Sea with Iceland was published 26 January 2026. *Source: BMV Homepage, Federal Ministry of Transport, 2026-01-26. https://bmdv.bund.de/EN/Home/home.html.

Analytical inference: Coordinated coastal state positions on shipping typically precede formal regulatory proposals at IMO or EU level. For workspaces with Baltic or North Sea port calls, this letter is an early indicator of potential future shore power, fuel quality, or emissions reporting requirements in those sea areas.

*Re-check window:* Monitor IMO and EU shipping policy developments through H2 2026.

---

# Substantive Requirements

*No content for this section as of 2026-05-27: The source blocks confirm the BMV's existence, leadership, and active policy signals, but do not provide text of any specific binding regulatory instrument with article-level obligations applicable to freight forwarders. Instrument-specific briefs (e.g., WasserstoffBG full text, ReFuelEU Aviation, IMO MEPC resolutions) should be sourced separately for substantive requirements analysis.*

The WasserstoffBG table of contents confirms the law contains provisions on: purpose and objectives (§1), scope of application (§2), definitions (§3), overriding public interest designation (§4), EIA modifications (§5), accelerated procurement (§6), accelerated review (§7), legal remedies (§8), jurisdictional competence of higher administrative courts and the Federal Administrative Court (§9), and transitional provisions (§10). *Source: WasserstoffBG, Gesetze im Internet, https://www.gesetze-im-internet.de/wasserstoffbg/.

*Specific [effective date / compliance deadline / penalty provision] for WasserstoffBG not available from primary sources as of 2026-05-27.* The source block provides a table of contents only; article text is not reproduced.

---

# Registration and Reporting Obligations

*No content for this section as of 2026-05-27: No registration or reporting obligations directly applicable to freight forwarders under BMV-administered instruments have been identified in the source blocks. Instrument-specific briefs are required.*

---

# Operational System Requirements

prior.full_brief:
# TxDOT Freight Planning: Statewide Framework and Resources

## Market Signal Brief

---

## 1. Purpose and Scope of This Document

This brief covers the Texas Department of Transportation (TxDOT) statewide freight planning framework as it exists and is being developed through 2027. Sources cover: the 2027 Texas Freight Mobility Plan (TFMP) development timeline, the Texas Connected Freight Corridors technology deployment programme, the Texas Emissions Reduction Plan (TERP) grant ecosystem, the U.S. DOT federal state-freight-plan registry entry for Texas, and the El Paso MPO's active participation in corridor designation processes. One source (the USDOT Carbon Reduction Strategies PDF) returned an access-denied response and contributes no grounded content to this brief.

The primary audience for this brief: freight forwarders and logistics operators with road-primary operations on Texas trade lanes, particularly those moving cargo through the Texas Triangle (Austin–Dallas-Fort Worth–Houston–San Antonio) or across the U.S.–Mexico border at Laredo or El Paso.

**Convention note:** All binding-modal assertions are either (a) quoted verbatim from a source block and cited inline, (b) prefixed with a label token, or (c) routed to *Legal Confirmation Required:*. Unlabeled analytical sentences are not present.

---

## 2. What's Moving and What Triggered It

**MONITORING**

TxDOT is in active development of its 2027 Texas Freight Mobility Plan (TFMP), described in the primary source as "the four-year update of its 2023 plan, in accordance with requirements in the Fixing America's Surface Transportation Act and 49 USC 70202." *Source: Texas Freight Mobility Plan, TxDOT, 2025–2026. https://www.txdot.gov/projects/planning/freight-planning/texas-freight-mobility-plan.html.

The published development schedule runs "Late 2025 through Late 2027 – Public involvement," with discrete phase milestones: "Late 2025 – Project kickoff, data assessment, analysis of trends, disruptors, and opportunities. Early 2026 – Multimodal data inventory, conduct a data needs assessment, supply chain review, critical freight facility assessment. Mid 2026 – Begin stakeholder engagement, conduct a freight bottleneck and network assessments. Late 2026 – Document economic importance of multimodal freight, finalize Texas freight mobility recommendations, finalize implementation plan." *Source: Texas Freight Mobility Plan, TxDOT, 2025–2026. https://www.txdot.gov/projects/planning/freight-planning/texas-freight-mobility-plan.html.

Analytical inference: The plan is now in the data assessment and multimodal inventory phase (late 2025–early 2026 window per the published schedule), meaning the public window for shaping freight bottleneck identification and supply chain review inputs is open now and will narrow significantly by mid-2026 when stakeholder engagement formalises.

The federal trigger is explicit: the FAST Act (49 USC 70202) drives the four-year planning cycle. The U.S. DOT's Office of Policy hosts the Texas state freight plan registry, with the most recently published plan document listed as "texas-delivers-2050.pdf," last updated December 6, 2023. *Source: Texas Freight Plan, U.S. Department of Transportation, December 6, 2023. https://www.transportation.gov/mission/office-secretary/office-policy/freight/freight-infrastructure-and-policy/texas-freight-plan.

Operational implication: The gap between the 2023 federal plan filing and the 2027 TFMP under development creates a documentation window. Operational data submitted during the 2027 TFMP process may influence both the state plan and the next federal plan update cycle.

---

## 3. Who's Driving It and What They Want

TxDOT is the lead agency. The TFMP is framed around Texas's stated economic position: "If Texas were a nation, it would be the 8th largest economy in the world. It has been the #1 U.S. exporter for 23 years in a row, and it is the largest energy-producing state in the Nation." *Source: Texas Freight Mobility Plan, TxDOT, 2025–2026. https://www.txdot.gov/projects/planning/freight-planning/texas-freight-mobility-plan.html.

Stakeholder engagement is structured around the Texas Freight Advisory Committee (TFAC), Metropolitan Planning Organization (MPO) freight advisory committees, "stakeholder workshops, interviews, project communications, surveys, and project webpage contents." *Source: Texas Freight Mobility Plan, TxDOT, 2025–2026. https://www.txdot.gov/projects/planning/freight-planning/texas-freight-mobility-plan.html.

The El Paso MPO illustrates active sub-state participation: "El Paso MPO staff is actively participating in the freight plans for New Mexico and Texas, including identifying critical corridors at state and local level. As part of the 2022 Texas Freight Plan development, MPO staff has submitted to TxDOT six proposed Critical Urban Freight Corridors (CUFC)." *Source: El Paso MPO – Freight, El Paso Metropolitan Planning Organization. https://www.elpasompo.org/Freight.

Analytical inference: MPO-level corridor submissions during prior plan cycles (2022) set a precedent for how border-region operators can influence the corridor designation process. Freight operators with significant El Paso or Laredo cross-border volume have a structural pathway — via their regional MPO — to propose or support CUFC designations in the 2027 TFMP cycle.

The Texas Connected Freight Corridors project identifies a distinct set of public–private stakeholders: "The Texas Connected Freight Corridors Project is a collaborative effort with public and private stakeholders to deploy connected vehicle technologies to more than 1,000 commercial vehicles." *Source: Texas Connected Freight Corridors, TxDOT Innovative Transportation. https://www.txdot.gov/about/programs/innovative-transportation/texas-connected-freight-corridors.html.

The Texas Commission on Environmental Quality (TCEQ) operates as a separate but parallel actor through the Texas Emissions Reduction Plan (TERP): "Since 2001, TERP has provided thousands of Texans with grant funding to purchase new vehicles and equipment." TERP's stated mission is to "cut NOX emissions that affect the air of our great state." *Source: Texas Emissions Reduction Plan, TCEQ, last modified 2026-06-03. https://www.tceq.texas.gov/airquality/terp.

---

## 4. Expected Trajectory and Conversion Triggers

The 2027 TFMP is on a published, legislatively mandated schedule. Conversion from planning signal to binding infrastructure and investment decisions occurs in phases:

- Late 2026: "finalize Texas freight mobility recommendations, finalize implementation plan" — this is the point at which project-level capital investment proposals solidify. *Source: Texas Freight Mobility Plan, TxDOT, 2025–2026. https://www.txdot.gov/projects/planning/freight-planning/texas-freight-mobility-plan.html.
- Late 2027: Full plan publication concludes the current cycle, per the stated "Late 2025 through Late 2027 – Public involvement" window. *Source: Texas Freight Mobility Plan, TxDOT, 2025–2026. https://www.txdot.gov/projects/planning/freight-planning/texas-freight-mobility-plan.html.

Analytical inference: The late 2026 recommendation finalization represents the critical conversion trigger. After that date, the plan's project priorities become the basis for TxDOT's capital programming, and influencing which corridors or bottlenecks receive attention becomes significantly harder.

The Texas Connected Freight Corridors project is framed as a technology transfer vehicle: "In the long term, TxDOT believes these applications can be transferred to other highway corridors where similar safety and mobility challenges exist." *Source: Texas Connected Freight Corridors, TxDOT Innovative Transportation. https://www.txdot.gov/about/programs/innovative-transportation/texas-connected-freight-corridors.html. Analytical inference: Corridors not yet in scope for connected vehicle deployment could be prioritised in the 2027 TFMP, making early engagement with the technology rollout a positioning opportunity rather than a compliance obligation.

TERP grant availability is cyclical and not permanently open: "There are no open grants at this time. Join the TERP Hot Topics email list to get updates on TERP programs." *Source: Texas Emissions Reduction Plan, TCEQ, last modified 2026-06-03. https://www.tceq.texas.gov/airquality/terp. Analytical inference: Grant windows open unpredictably; fleet operators without email alert subscriptions risk missing competitive funding cycles for truck repowering or replacement.

---

## 5. Operational and Cost Implications If It Materialises

Filtered by road-primary operations, Texas Triangle and U.S.–Mexico border trade lanes:

**Connected vehicle deployment on the Texas Triangle:**
The Texas Connected Freight Corridors project "covers the 865-mile Texas Triangle of I-35, including extension to Laredo, I-45, and I-10 linking Austin, Dallas-Fort Worth, Houston, and San Antonio." *Source: Texas Connected Freight Corridors, TxDOT Innovative Transportation. https://www.txdot.gov/about/programs/innovative-transportation/texas-connected-freight-corridors.html.

Operational implication: For road-primary operators with high-frequency Texas Triangle movements, the connected vehicle technology aims to "reduce the time trucks spend in congestion" and enable "vehicle interaction with traffic signals to reduce stops and truck idling time." These are direct dwell-time and fuel-cost variables. Current rate of reduction not publicly available from these source blocks.

Operational implication: The Laredo extension of I-35 is explicitly within the project's geographic scope, making this directly relevant to operators managing cross-border U.S.–Mexico freight flows.

**TERP fleet upgrade grants:**
TERP "offers grants for people and businesses in industries like trucking, farming, and construction" and "to date, TERP has distributed over $1.4 billion in grants to reduce NOX emissions across Texas." *Source: Texas Emissions Reduction Plan, TCEQ, last modified 2026-06-03. https://www.tceq.texas.gov/airquality/terp.

Operational implication: Road freight operators running older heavy-duty equipment on Texas corridors — particularly those in counties with air quality non-attainment designations — have access to grant funding that directly offsets truck replacement or repowering costs. The Diesel Emissions Reduction Incentive (DERI) Rebate Grant Program is specifically listed for "On-Road Haul Truck" and "Tractor trailers." *Source: Texas Emissions Reduction Plan, TCEQ, last modified 2026-06-03. https://www.tceq.texas.gov/airquality/terp.

**CUFC designation consequences:**
Analytical inference: Critical Urban Freight Corridor designation, as used in the 2022 Texas Freight Plan cycle and as the El Paso MPO process illustrates, influences which routes receive federal freight formula funds under FAST Act apportionments. Operators with distribution infrastructure on designated corridors benefit from preferential capital investment; those on undesignated routes do not.

*Legal Confirmation Required:* whether freight forwarders or contract logistics operators have standing to submit corridor nomination inputs directly to TxDOT or exclusively through MPO channels in the 2027 TFMP stakeholder process — this turns on TxDOT's engagement plan terms, which have not been published in final form in these source blocks.

---

## 6. Competitive Implications

**No named competitor positions were identified in the source blocks.** The sources describe the public planning framework and technology programme; they do not contain operator-level competitive positioning data.

Analytical inference: Freight operators that engage directly in the TFMP stakeholder process — through the Texas Freight Advisory Committee, MPO freight advisory committees, or the stakeholder workshops referenced in the plan — gain first-view access to bottleneck assessment findings and project priority rankings before they are finalised in late 2026. This is an informational advantage over operators who wait for the published plan.

Analytical inference: For workspaces managing road freight through the Texas Triangle at scale, being among the "more than 1,000 commercial vehicles" participating in the Texas Connected Freight Corridors deployment provides early access to real-time corridor condition data and signal-priority technology that non-participating fleets do not receive. The programme is framed as a collaborative public–private effort; the pathway to participation is through the TxDOT freight contact channel (TxDOT_Freight@txdot.gov, 737-219-9044).

Analytical inference: TERP grant access is competitive and first-come or competitive-basis depending on programme: the Texas Natural Gas Vehicle Grant Program (TNGVGP) is "first-come, first-served," while the Emissions Reduction Incentive Grants Program (ERIG) is "competitive basis." *Source: Texas Emissions Reduction Plan, TCEQ, last modified 2026-06-03. https://www.tceq.texas.gov/airquality/terp. Operators who subscribe to TERP grant alerts and have fleet upgrade plans ready to submit are positioned ahead of those who learn of grant openings reactively.

---

## 7. Client Conversation Talking Points

**What the workspace can credibly say:**

- Texas is the largest U.S. export state and would rank as the 8th largest economy globally if independent — a fact from TxDOT's own planning documents that contextualises the strategic importance of Texas freight infrastructure investment. *Source: Texas Freight Mobility Plan, TxDOT. https://www.txdot.gov/projects/planning/freight-planning/texas-freight-mobility-plan.html.
- TxDOT is currently building its 2027 Freight Mobility Plan, with public involvement open through late 2027. Clients with Texas supply chains can provide input during the stakeholder engagement phase (mid-2026 onward per the published schedule).
- The Texas Connected Freight Corridors programme is deploying connected vehicle technology across the full I-35 / I-45 / I-10 Texas Triangle, including the Laredo extension. This is directly relevant to clients with cross-border U.S.–Mexico supply chains.
- TERP grant funding for truck fleet upgrades is available — over $1.4 billion distributed to date — but grant windows are not permanent. Clients running older heavy-duty equipment in Texas should be tracking TERP cycles.

**Claims to avoid:**

- Do not assert specific congestion reduction percentages or emissions savings from the Texas Connected Freight Corridors programme — no performance outcomes are reported in the sourced content.
- Do not assert that the 2027 TFMP has been published or that its project priorities have been finalised. As of the published schedule, recommendations are not expected until late 2026.
- Do not claim that TERP grants are currently open. The TCEQ page states no grants are open at the time of the source retrieval.
- Do not assert specific CUFC designations for corridors outside El Paso unless sourced from other MPO submissions.

**Questions to pose to clients:**

- Which Texas corridors carry your highest freight volume, and are they currently on the Primary Highway Freight System (PHFS) or a designated CUFC?
- Is your Texas-based trucking fleet eligible for DERI rebate grants? When did you last assess TERP programme eligibility?
- Has your regional MPO submitted corridor proposals for the 2027 TFMP cycle?

---

## 8. What the Workspace Should Do Now

**ACTION REQUIRED — mid-2026 stakeholder window**

1. **Register with TxDOT freight planning contacts before the mid-2026 stakeholder engagement phase opens.** Contact: TxDOT_Freight@txdot.gov, 737-219-9044. The published schedule shows stakeholder engagement beginning mid-2026; pre-registration positions the workspace to receive invitations to workshops and surveys that shape the bottleneck and network assessments.

2. **Subscribe to TERP Hot Topics email alerts immediately.** TERP grant cycles open without advance notice. The DERI Rebate Grant Program directly covers on-road haul trucks and tractor-trailers. No grants are currently open, making now the right time to prepare eligibility documentation. Contact: terp@tceq.texas.gov, 800-919-TERP (8377). *Source: Texas Emissions Reduction Plan, TCEQ. https://www.tceq.texas.gov/airquality/terp.

3. **Assess fleet composition against TERP programme criteria.** Operational implication: Workspaces running older diesel heavy-duty vehicles in Texas non-attainment counties should conduct a fleet audit now to determine DERI eligibility — so applications can be submitted immediately when a grant window opens.

4. **Engage the regional MPO on CUFC submission status for trade-critical corridors.** As the El Paso MPO example demonstrates, MPO-level submissions shape which corridors receive federal freight funding. For workspaces with significant El Paso, Laredo, or other border-point volume, engagement with the relevant MPO's freight advisory committee before formal TFMP stakeholder engagement begins (mid-2026) is the correct timing.

5. **Monitor the Texas Connected Freight Corridors programme for fleet participation pathways.** The programme targets "more than 1,000 commercial vehicles." Operational implication: if the workspace operates trucks on the Texas Triangle at scale, there may be a participation pathway. The programme design (safety notifications, signal priority near distribution centres) has direct operational benefit for dwell time and safety compliance management.

6. **Set a re-check window: late 2026.** The TFMP recommendation finalization and implementation plan are scheduled for late 2026. The brief should be refreshed at that point to assess which projects and corridors were prioritised and what that means for the workspace's Texas infrastructure planning.

---

## New Sources Identified

|---|---|---|---|
| Texas Freight Mobility Plan (TxDOT) | https://www.txdot.gov/projects/planning/freight-planning/texas-freight-mobility-plan.html | 2 | Primary state agency source for the 2027 TFMP development schedule, stakeholder engagement structure, and planned outcomes — foundational for all timeline claims. |
| Texas Connected Freight Corridors (TxDOT) | https://www.txdot.gov/about/programs/innovative-transportation/texas-connected-freight-corridors.html | 2 | Primary state agency source for the connected vehicle deployment scope, corridor coverage, and programme goals — grounds all CV technology claims. |
| Texas Emissions Reduction Plan (TCEQ) | https://www.tceq.texas.gov/airquality/terp | 2 | Primary state regulator source for TERP grant programmes, fleet upgrade funding, and NOX reduction context — grounds fleet cost-reduction claims. |
| Texas Freight Plan (U.S. DOT) | https://www.transportation.gov/mission/office-secretary/office-policy/freight/freight-infrastructure-and-policy/texas-freight-plan | 2 | Federal registry entry confirming federal plan filing (texas-delivers-2050.pdf, December 2023) and FAST Act mandate context. |
| El Paso MPO – Freight | https://www.elpasompo.org/Freight | 3 | Sub-state MPO source illustrating the CUFC nomination process and binational border freight corridor coordination — grounds the border-region corridor engagement pathway. |
